title: How Cats See the World 🐱 UV and Night Vision
description: Cats see ultraviolet light, detect movement at speeds imperceptible to humans, and their night vision is six times better than ours. Discover what the world really looks like through a cat's eyes according to science. Data from universities and published studies on feline vision.

segments.voice: Your cat can see things that are completely invisible to you. Researchers from the University of London demonstrated in 2014 that cats perceive ultraviolet light, a spectrum that humans cannot detect. Cats literally see a broader world than we do. What they see every day would be invisible to us.
segments.visual: cat eyes glowing in dim light close up

segments.voice: Welcome to CatBrothers. Today, we're going to put on a cat's eyes and discover how they really see the world. Spoiler: it's not like documentaries show. Feline vision is a system completely different from humans', optimized for night hunting and motion detection. Let's debunk everything you think you know.
segments.visual: cat looking at camera mysterious eyes

segments.voice: Let's start with the eye structure. A cat's eye is proportionally enormous: it occupies about twenty percent of its skull. If our eyes were proportionally the same, they'd be the size of tennis balls. Dr. Heather Lewis, a veterinary ophthalmologist at the University of Wisconsin, explains that this size allows them to capture much more light.
segments.visual: cat eye extreme macro close up pupil

segments.voice: The feline retina has a radically different ratio of rods to cones compared to humans. Cats have twenty-five rods for every cone, while humans have four rods per cone. Rods detect light and motion. Cones detect color. According to research from the University of Bristol, this allows cats to see six times better than us in low-light conditions.
segments.visual: cat eyes reflecting light in darkness

segments.voice: The tapetum lucidum is the key to night vision. It's a reflective layer behind the retina that acts like a mirror, bouncing back light that wasn't captured so the retina gets a second chance to process it. Dr. Ron Ofri from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem measured that the tapetum lucidum doubles the amount of light available to photoreceptors.
segments.visual: cat eyes shining bright in dark

segments.voice: That eerie glow you see in your cat's eyes when you shine a light on them in the dark is exactly the tapetum lucidum reflecting light back. The color of the reflection depends on eye color: green in green-eyed cats, gold in amber-eyed cats, and red in Siamese cats that lack a tapetum.
segments.visual: multiple cat eyes glowing green dark

segments.voice: A cat's pupils are a masterpiece of optical engineering. They can dilate to occupy ninety percent of the iris, capturing maximum light. And they contract into a vertical slit as thin as a needle. A 2015 study from the University of California, Berkeley published in Science Advances showed that vertical pupils allow light control three hundred times greater than human round pupils.
segments.visual: cat pupil dilating contracting close up

segments.voice: Why vertical and not round? Researcher Martin Banks from Berkeley discovered that vertical pupils are an adaptation of low-stature ambush predators. The vertical slit optimizes horizontal depth perception, exactly what a crouching cat needs to calculate the jumping distance toward prey moving laterally.
segments.visual: cat crouching ready to pounce hunt

segments.voice: Now for the most major topic: ultraviolet vision. A 2014 study by Ronald Douglas and Glen Jeffery from the University of London published in Proceedings of the Royal Society B showed that a cat's lens transmits ultraviolet light up to 317 nanometers. The human lens blocks UV. Cats see a spectrum that doesn't exist for us.
segments.visual: ultraviolet light flowers glowing pattern

segments.voice: What can cats see in UV that we can't? Many things. Rodent urine glows brightly under UV light. Other cats' territorial markings too. Certain patterns on prey fur and flowers are only visible in UV. An entire world of signals invisible to the human eye is available to your cat every day.
segments.visual: glowing uv light patterns nature

segments.voice: As for color, cats don't see in black and white as once believed, but they don't see like us either. They have two types of cones, sensitive to blue and green. Humans have three, adding red. A University of California study published in Visual Neuroscience showed cats see blues and yellows clearly but don't distinguish reds and greens well.
segments.visual: color spectrum comparison cat human vision

segments.voice: A cat's color vision is comparable to a person with deuteranopia, a type of red-green color blindness. The world to a cat is a palette of blues, yellows, muted greens, and grays. The red of a toy looks like a grayish brown. That's why blue and yellow toys are more stimulating to cats than red ones.
segments.visual: colorful cat toys blue yellow selection

segments.voice: Motion detection is where feline vision far surpasses humans'. Cats can detect movement at speeds as slow as two millimeters per second, according to MIT research. Humans need at least ten times that speed. This explains why a cat spots an insect moving on the wall that you don't even notice.
segments.visual: cat watching tiny insect on wall alert

segments.voice: However, cats' static vision is inferior to humans'. Cat visual acuity is approximately 20/100 to 20/200 on the human scale, according to Dr. Kerry Ketring from the American College of Veterinary Ophthalmology. A cat needs to be six meters away to see clearly what a human sees at twenty meters. If it's not moving, a cat might not see it well.
segments.visual: cat squinting trying to focus far

segments.voice: A cat's visual field is wider than humans': two hundred degrees versus our one hundred eighty. But the binocular field, where both eyes overlap to create depth perception, is only one hundred forty degrees versus our one hundred twenty. A University of Cambridge study showed this configuration optimizes both peripheral vision and distance perception.
segments.visual: cat scanning environment wide eyes

segments.voice: Cats have a third eyelid called the nictitating membrane. This semi-transparent membrane slides horizontally to protect and lubricate the eye without completely blocking vision. According to Dr. Cynthia Powell from the Colorado State University College of Veterinary Medicine, the third eyelid contains a tear gland that produces up to fifty percent of a cat's tears.
segments.visual: cat third eyelid visible close up

segments.voice: Cats blink much less than humans. While we blink fifteen to twenty times per minute, cats blink two to five times. The slow blink has a social meaning documented by the University of Sussex: it's a trust signal. When your cat slow-blinks at you, it's saying it feels safe with you.
segments.visual: cat slow blinking at owner trust

segments.voice: A cat's flicker fusion rate is about seventy hertz, compared to humans' sixty hertz. This means cats perceive old TV screens as a series of flickering images. Modern high-frequency LED screens are smoother for cats, which is why some react to current screens.
segments.visual: cat watching television screen curious

segments.voice: Cat eyes have a little-known additional reflective structure. Purkinje fibers at the retina's periphery are extremely sensitive to motion. A study from Japan's National Institute of Optical Research showed these fibers allow cats to detect movement in their peripheral vision with four hundred percent greater efficiency than humans.
segments.visual: cat turning head quickly detecting movement

segments.voice: Cat myopia makes evolutionary sense. As ambush predators, cats need to see nearby objects—prey—in maximum detail. Blurry distance vision isn't a disadvantage because they hunt by pouncing, not long-distance chasing. The feline visual system sacrificed far clarity for maximum efficiency at hunting distances: one to six meters.
segments.visual: cat pouncing on toy precise close range

segments.voice: Cat eye color is genetically determined by melanin. Blue eyes have little melanin, green eyes a medium amount, and amber or copper eyes have much. White cats with blue eyes have higher rates of deafness: a Cornell University study found sixty-five to eighty-five percent are deaf in at least one ear.
segments.visual: white cat with blue eyes beautiful

segments.voice: Heterochromia—having one eye of each color—is relatively common in cats. It especially affects white breeds like the Turkish Van and Turkish Angora. A University of Leipzig study showed cats with heterochromia are often deaf on the blue-eyed side, as the lack of melanin affects both the iris and cochlea on that side.
segments.visual: cat with heterochromia different eye colors

segments.voice: Kittens are born with closed eyes and open them between seven and ten days old. All kittens are born with blue eyes because they lack melanin. The final color is established between six and eight weeks of age according to the Veterinary Ophthalmology Association. In the first weeks, their vision is blurry and they don't perceive depth.
segments.visual: tiny kitten opening eyes first time

segments.voice: In summary: your cat sees ultraviolet light, detects movements imperceptible to you, has night vision six times better than yours, controls light three hundred times better with its pupils, and has a two-hundred-degree visual field. In exchange, it sees limited colors and distant objects blurry. Evolution didn't create perfect eyes but perfect eyes for hunting.
segments.visual: cat eyes beautiful dramatic lighting

segments.voice: If feline vision blew your mind, subscribe to CatBrothers now and hit that bell. The next and final episode of this season will reveal something striking: meows are a language cats invented exclusively to communicate with humans. They don't use it with each other. It's remarkable. Don't miss it.
segments.visual: happy cat waving paw at camera
